title: Complex Magnetic Behavior of the Ce sawtooth chains in CeRhSn$_2$
authors: P. Opletal, J. Fikáček, E. Duverger-Nédellec, A. Thamizhavel, Z. Hossain, R. Tarasenko, V. Tkáč, D. Legut, Bin Shen, P. Gegenwart, J. Custers
abstract: Conflicting reports exist on the ground state of the intermetallic compound CeRhSn$_2$. This can be rooted in the sawtooth-like arrangement of two inequivalent Ce sites in the unit cell, which suggests potential geometric magnetic frustration. To resolve, we conducted a comprehensive study on high-quality single crystals of CeRhSn$_2$ by means of magnetization ($M$), specific heat ($C_p/T$), and resistivity ($ρ$). The system exhibits strong magnetic anisotropy, confirming the $b$-axis as the easy magnetic axis. We establish three successive transitions, an AFM order at $T_{N} = 3.65$K, a first-order FM order at $T_{C} = 1.7$K and final transition, at $T = 1.5$K. The transition temperatures are highly field-directional dependent: in a magnetic field, the lowest transition is immediately suppressed while $\mathbf{H} \parallel b$ rapidly merges $T_{C}$ and $T_{N}$ into a single second-order transition. Conversely, $\mathbf{H}\parallel c$ suppresses the FM order and reduces $T_{N}$. Additional ab initio calculations affirm the FM ground state of CeRhSn$_2$. The observation of an enhancement of the Sommerfeld coefficient ($γ= 76.5$mJ/mol$\cdot$K$^2$) may arise from geometric frustration, but it is most consistently attributed to weak Kondo hybridization as frustration cannot be conclusively established through our data.
summary: This work investigates the complex magnetic behavior of CeRhSn2, a material with a geometrically frustrated structure. By combining detailed measurements of magnetization, specific heat, and resistivity with DFT calculations, the authors resolve conflicting reports to establish a clear sequence of magnetic transitions. They conclude that the material exhibits a ferromagnetic ground state, while attributing the large electronic specific heat coefficient to weak Kondo hybridization.
main_problem: The paper aims to resolve conflicting reports regarding the ground state of the intermetallic compound CeRhSn2, which is complicated by a sawtooth-like arrangement of Ce sites suggesting magnetic frustration.
main_result: The study establishes three successive magnetic transitions (AFM at 3.65 K, FM at 1.7 K, and a final transition at 1.5 K) and concludes that the system possesses a ferromagnetic ground state, despite initial signs of frustration.
method: The research combines extensive experimental measurements (magnetization, specific heat, resistivity) on high-quality single crystals with ab initio Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is the intermetallic compound CeRhSn2, which has an orthorhombic structure and exhibits complex magnetic anisotropy due to its crystal structure and electronic interactions.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), specific heat divided by temperature (Cp/T), resistivity (rho), and magnetic transition temperatures (TN, TC).
important_parameters: Sommerfeld coefficient (gamma = 76.5 mJ/mol·K^2), magnetic anisotropy (b-axis easy axis), and critical temperatures (3.65 K, 1.7 K, 1.5 K).
main_assumptions: The authors assume that the observed magnetic transitions and thermodynamic anomalies provide definitive evidence for the magnetic ground state, while acknowledging that the enhancement of gamma might be due to Kondo hybridization rather than frustration.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the crystal structure showing two inequivalent Ce sites and the sawtooth pattern; magnetization curves show field-dependent transitions; and Cp/T vs. T plots reveal anomalies corresponding to the magnetic ordering temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper follows a structure of literature review/problem statement, detailed experimental characterization across multiple physical properties (M, Cp, rho), analysis of field and temperature dependencies to pinpoint multiple transitions, and finally, theoretical calculations to confirm the magnetic ground state.
why_interesting: The study provides a detailed, multi-faceted investigation into magnetic ordering in a heavy fermion material, directly addressing the interplay between crystal structure, magnetic frustration, and electronic correlations, which is central to strongly correlated electron physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Picture of the crystal structure of CeRhSn2 empha- sizing (a) the two inequivalent Ce sites. The view from above of the bc plane (b) shows the magnetic lattice of CeRhSn2. Ce2 (Wyckoﬀposition 4a) sites align in linear chains along the c-axis and link with Ce1 (Wyckoﬀposition 4c) sites to create a pattern of alternating sawtooth-shaped isosceles tri- angles. The shortest Ce2–Ce2 distance (c) is along the a–axis.

FIG. 2. Evolution of the magnetization M at low temperature and in an applied ﬁeld of µ0H = 0.01 T. Closed and open symbols denote zero-ﬁeld-cooled (ZFC) and ﬁeld-cooled (FC) measurements, respectively, with subscripts indicating sample numbers. The red lines are low-temperature Curie-Weiss ﬁts (4 < T < 10 K). Measurements T < 2 K (T ≥1.8) K in the orientation H ∥b were carried out on sample #5 (#1). The inset plots the inverse of magnetic susceptibility χ−1 = H/M vs temperature T in a ﬁeld of µ0H = 1 T along the a axis (black circle), b axis (green circle), and c axis (blue circle).The red lines represent Curie-Weiss ﬁts applied to the data within the temperature range of 200 < T < 300 K.

FIG. 3. Field dependence of the magnetization for ﬁeld ap- plied a, b, and c axis measured at 1.8 K and at T = 0.45 K (∥b). The inset shows a zoom-in of the low-ﬁeld region of M vs H. Closed (open) symbols show the data in increasing (decreasing) ﬁeld. The data ∥a and ∥c overlap in low ﬁelds. A ﬁnite M at zero ﬁeld persists along b in the T = 0.45 K measurement.
FIG. 4. Magnetic ﬁeld isotherms of CeRhSn2 at various temperatures for H parallel (a) a axis, (b) b axis, and (c) c axis, respectively. Open (closed) symbols depict ZFC (FC) results.

FIG. 5. The heat capacity Cp divided by temperature T vs T in various ﬁelds applied parallel b-axis. Open (closed) sym- bols is in cooling down (warming up) cycle. The upper inset enlarges the region around the double transition in zero ﬁeld and from a second sample (#2). The lower inset presents the ﬁt to the data using Eq. 1.
